speaker: interviewer
text: November 11, 1996. The survivor is Maya Schwartz. Helga Weinbach, interviewer. It's in Los Angeles, California, USA, to be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Helga Weinbach, and today is November 11, 1996. I'm interviewing Maya Schwartz in Los Angeles, California. Good morning, Maya. Just to begin with, state your name, and spell it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name right now is Maya Schwartz, M-A-Y-A Schwartz, S-C-H-W-A-R-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Finkel, F-I-N-K-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And so today you are? Your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age today, I am 64 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and the country in which you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paris, France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. To begin with, I'd like to ask you a little bit about your family, what you remember of your mother, your father. and give us their names, and what your family life was like before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Rachel-- Rachelle Leah. And my father was Samuel Mendel Finkel. My mother was called Rochele. And my father was called Shmiele, sometimes Mendel and Finkel. And I grew up in a working class suburb of Paris, called Clichy. And we had a very good life. We-- we My parents were humble folks. And on my birth certificate, they are listed as factory workers. And I remember my father being very militant and always speaking words like revolution, and justice, and-- and freedom, and democracy. And I believe he was a union organizer. And he had told me that he had been in trouble in the past for activities he had organized, a demonstration in favor of Sacco and Vanzetti and other things. And anyway, my father was lots of fun. He-- he was very [SOBBING] affectionate, and very dedicated to my mother and myself. And we had wonderful friends. And I remember just about every friend being a tailor or a seamstress. And on Sunday, we would visit them. And many of them lived in a one room apartment on the sixth floor. And that room was divided by a divider. And there were a couple sewing machines, and clothes hanging all over the place to be finished, buttonholes, zippers, coats. And they would-- while we children played-- the-- the grownups would be sewing by hand or on the machine. And when that was over-- oh, I just want to mention, there was one toilet downstairs that served six floors of apartments. And we had to prepare. We would go downstairs with a book and wait in line. And so, [LAUGHS] we got used to it. And then afterwards we would often go to the Jardin du Luxembourg. It's a famous garden in Paris. And all of us kids ran around, while and our parents would talk. Usually they enjoyed talking politics. And then we'd go back home and eat. And they often played cards or dominoes. And we often had people come over. And we'd come over there. And sometimes when the last metro had been gone, we would sleep over people's house, or they would sleep in our house. Everybody get in one big bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember a very joyful family. And I-- I worked very hard at school. I was a very good student. So my parents expected me to be a good student. A little bit more about your parents. Where did they originally come from? Where they French? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were born in Poland. My father came from Brody. And my mom was born in Warsaw. And I understand they, as youngsters, there was a-- a youth movement-- Zionist movement to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think they were both around 16, 17, they decided to leave their hometown with friends. And they settled, what was then called Palestine. And I was told that my father met my mother on the boat. She was 16. And they fell in love, and she married against her mother's wish. Ah, they married in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: They married in Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they come back to Europe and to--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father would say that, at the time, France represented to him the land of freedom and opportunity. I guess he had read about the French Revolution. And he was familiar with the works of Voltaire, and Rousseau, and other French revolutionaries. And I believe there was Leon Blum and the Front populaire. They did a great deal of work in terms of social security, child welfare, helping out communities, creating parks and playgrounds. And bread lines during the war there were, and before the war. There was-- here we call it welfare. But in those days, it was called [NON-ENGLISH], help to needy families. And we were fitted for used clothing twice a year, once in the summer, once in the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you-- what did your parents do when they came to--

speaker: interviewee
text: They worked in factories. I understand at one time, they worked in a paper factory, and another time, at a beer factory. And then my father, when the war began, my father joined what was called [NON-ENGLISH], which was the foreign army. And he escaped from that camp. And that was a mistake he made, because by escaping-- had he not escaped from that camp, he would have been a regular prisoner, who are protected by the Convention of Geneva. But he escaped, and then later on he was arrested as a Jew. And Jews who were arrested and interned were not protected under any international convention whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's just step back. In other words, your father was in the French Army. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was captured.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. I see. What were your first memories of the war itself as a child? That you knew the war had broken out, and it was different. What do you remember of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] I remember everything. I have-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I could talk about all the details. I remember very, very sharply everything. [SOBBING] And that was-- I was at camp. I was seven years old, and I didn't want to go to camp. And that child-- you know, children's camp-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- I wasn't ready for it. And my father came to visit me. And when I came home from that camp, life was never have the same again. He had gone to the army. And so from that moment on, it was my mother and me. And she made a living by cleaning wealthy people's homes and sewing and bringing home sometimes the ironing and the sewing. And I remember she sewed buttons on soldiers' uniforms and just any sewing that needed to be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel different as a Jew? Or, how did you feel different? Did your classmates treat you differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. I think I was always aware that I was different, you know, because my parents spoke Yiddish at home. And they had a thick foreign accent when they spoke French. And they sometimes spoke incorrectly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as a child, I had that feeling of embarrassment. And we were poor. However, everyone in our neighborhood was poor. We all saw each other on the bread line over in City Hall to be outfitted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So what was your question? How did you feel different? Or, did you feel different being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that, when my father was arrested as a Jew, and I think prior to that-- I can't pinpoint the date. From the moment that my mother sewed [SOBBING] the Jewish star, the Jewish yellow star on our clothing, the two of us and all our Jewish friend had to go out to wear that yellow star, with the word [NON-ENGLISH], Jew, I think from that moment on, my being part of French civilization kind of set me apart. [SOBBING] I-- I used to walk with my head down. Although, I must say that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very fortunate. Because our Jewish-- non-Jewish friends and neighbors were always very kind to us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just talk a little bit about your schooling and your religious background. What kind of religious background did you have, and what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an atheist. And as I said, he was militant. My mother did not discuss religion. I have a brother who is still living in Israel. He is a very religious Jew. And my aunt from America was-- she was semi-religious. She celebrated the Sabbath and holiday. And I was told there were rabbis on my mother's side. But-- but I was not brought up as a religious Jew whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you had this yellow star, describe a day as this child. How old were you? Do you remember how old you were when you had this yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably around eight. I would like to mention one thing that stands very vivid in my memory. And that is-- well, I remember my mother just breaking into [SOBBING] tears with the word, [NON-ENGLISH]. The war she remembered, painful memories of World War I. And I remember we were on the Champs-Elysees when the German soldiers paraded there. And they threw candy at all the children there. And one French lady said, [FRENCH] They're nice, the German. And one other person says, wait a while, you're going to see how really nice they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING FRENCH] So-- Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this person knew it meant death, death, and-- and hardship, and torture, and starvation, and whatever war brings about. So basically my days before the war-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the moment the war came and especially when my father-- was either in the army, or in the internment camp in France-- our life started losing balance. Because we were no longer a family. And since father wasn't there to make a living, my mother had to work. Around the clock she worked, long hours and brought work home, so that I felt neglected. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was angry at my mother, [SOBBING] and I gave her a hard time. And I have-- I have deep remorse about it. But I was a young child. I didn't know better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think the whole purpose of the Nazi strategy, number one, was to arrest the men to deprive the family of the father and husband. And then with the family just the mother and children, then it was easy to take care of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that your father was in internment camp. Was he allowed out? Did you ever-- did you hear from him? Was there correspondence?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father wrote regularly. And I believe we had one visit. There may have been more, but there's one that I remember. And it was the final one. And it was the last one I ever saw my father again. And there were French internment camps for Jews as well as for non-Jews throughout France and throughout Europe before of the death camps came along. And so we visited him. And it was the camp of Beuane-la-Rolande in the middle of France. And I remember that visit very well. And my mother had knitted things for him. She was always knitting and sewing. So she knitted a head cover, and-- and shawl, and gloves, and socks for him. And she had all sorts of things. And there's a picture of my father. They would bandage their knees, because their clothes would get raggedy. And she was a tiny woman. And she dragged all those packages of food and all the things [SOBBING] she had made. And we arrived. And it was a very desolate place. It was surrounded by barbed wires. [SOBBING] I was 10. 9, I was 9. Barbed wires and sentinels, guards with guns all over the place. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then, I think there was like a whistle blow and all the families got in. And we got to visit. I don't remember how long the visit lasted, so I snuggled up to my father. And I had brought him a book to read. I smuggled into the package one of my favorite books. And I snuggled up to him. And then he and my mother talked. And I-- they wanted to be alone a few minutes. And then I went back. And then there was whistle blow, [SOBBING] and we were parted. And-- and I remember my mother [CRYING] and myself hanging on to my father and every family just hanging on. And they had to tear us away. [SOBBING] And it was the last time I saw my father. Now, it-- shortly afterwards, my mother received a letter-- a card-- in German. She read it to me. My mother knew German. And it was from the Commander [INAUDIBLE]. And it said, your husband has been transferred. Expect to hear from him soon. And we never heard from him again. I just want to mention also, she received a postcard from my father's mother, who was in Poland at the time. And the postcard was written in Spanish. And we both cried-- it was written in Polish. And we both cried. And the postcard said, we are suffering so much. We are hungry all the time, and we're cold. And we don't know [SOBBING] what the future will hold. And we, my mother and I cried, when we got that postcard. And we never heard from her again. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: In the time that you saw that your-- you saw your father in this internment camp, and you got the note from the-- the commander of this, the German commander-- what did you-- did you plan on leaving with your mother ever? Did your mother have any ideas of escaping?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. There was a time. I think, at the beginning of the-- at the outbreak of war, people were kind of running all over the place. They'd pack a suitcase and maybe go in the free zone, or-- they were running.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember my mother packing a suitcase for her and for me, and we were in the subway station. And I said, well, mother, where are we running to? And she says, I don't know, I'm just running away. And I said, well, what's the point? And so, she says, yes. It was really no point, so we went back home. And you see those people running in the famous movie by Rene Clement, And all these refugees on the road, and the German bombs all over the place and people dying while escaping. And that's what you felt like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was also a moment. They asked us-- I think it was in school or from a social worker-- if I wanted to go to Switzerland. And I said, no. I wanted to be with my mother. And later on, when my mother was arrested and deported, I sometimes regretted. [SOBBING] I thought maybe had I gone to Switzerland, maybe her life would have been spared. But you know, it's-- it's hard to make sound decisions in those situations at that age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to leave my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, of course. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This incident where you were offered to-- to go to Switzerland and that you want-- wanted to stay with your mother, what happened next, before your mother? You said your mother was taken away from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the series of events before that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, I think-- Well, I remember-- and you are a historian, so there are books written about the whole-- as a matter of fact, I think they had a minister of Jewish culture, which sounds wonderful, you know. The-- its-- but it sounds like, you know, something good's about to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: But basically, this Ministry of German culture, under Marshal Petain and Laval, was to keep track of all the Jews. And eventually-- their-- their secret hidden goal was to deport them and liquidate them. But it started with you had to register as a Jew, just like we registered as a Republicans or Democrats. But what was your question? You said that your mother was taken away from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, I know. OK. Basically, what happened is that little by little, we were more and more and more restricted. So my-- I don't know which came first, the yellow star, or my father's arrest. But after that, Jews were not to go to concerts, museums, or cinema. It was forbidden for them to attend those events. Then it was forbidden to be out, or to take trains, or subways, or buses at certain time of the day. Then there was a curfew. And you had to be home at night. So there was this sneaking in of endless constrictions. So that was the whole idea, to trap us, so they knew what time we'd be home. And they could catch our-- as a matter of fact, one of the letters on the document, by Bousquet who was-- just lately, last year or so-- he was arrested, and he was shot, quote, 'accidentally.' But people know that he wouldn't have to talk. But one of the letters from the Klarsfeld files, by Bousquet, is make the Jews believe that in the free zone they are free. And when they least expect it, in the middle of the night, sneak in on them and arrest them. And they had a strong desire for efficiency, which is, arrest as many as you can in as short a time as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you as a child, in-- in-- in your vision as a-- as a 10-year-old that you were, 10, 11-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] describe the-- the-- the incidents that led to your mother being taken away from you. What-- what actually happened to you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your mother. How did that go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is one of the most painful--

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: --moments in my life. And-- and it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want a glass of water?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it had a-- [DRINKS] [SOBBING] it changed my life forever. And that was that my mother saved my life. And so I owe my life to my mother. And, [SIGHS] well, what happened was that they had what they called [FRENCH] raffle. And the word raffle means like a lottery.

speaker: interviewee
text: It has an ironical twist to it. But my mother was working for wealthy Jewish families. And that day when I came home from school, she was there. And I said, mama, why are you home so early?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says, the lady I work for said that there are rumors that they will arrest men, women, and children tomorrow, and-- and I'd better hide. Not to work today, and we're all going into hiding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- she said, in the evening, we will spend the night in the closet. And she says, and you must not talk. And we won't say anything. And hopefully, they won't break the door. We're taking a chance. A closet in your own apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a little closet. And it was next to that door. And it just had enough room for a broom, and maybe an ironing board and some old stuff, you know, you just throw in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what we did. We spent the night in that closet. And in the morning, they did come. But you were still in the closet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think they came about at 6:00 AM. And they knocked at the door, we heard. You know that-- what seemed to be an endless knock and voices. And just heard-- I mean, it just seemed to be forever. We held our breath. And we were hugging each other. [SOBBING] And then they went away. And so it's-- I call it [FRENCH], this moment of silence where my mother had prepared me. And she saved my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And afterwards, we decided to hide. We couldn't stay in this apartment. And we had very kind neighbors. And we hid in their homes. And I think there were other people who helped us. We just would go from one home to another and hide. And it was very scary. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There are some tissues there, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was very, very scary. Because we were no longer safe in our home. And when we went in the streets, there were police and Gestapo all over the place, stopping people, asking for identification. And so to always to be hiding and don't know when they're going to arrest you and jump on you is awfully scary. There was no more a safe place--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever interrogated with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm very fortunate in that I was spared the horror of arrest and deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in hiding with these various Christian neighbors, I assume.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what was the next step?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the next steps, my mother and I talked it over. And I was only 10 years old, but circumstances forced me to grow up fast. And so I suggested to my mother that maybe she could put me in a-- with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't we hold this and you'll-- for the next tape. Because we're running out of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'll tell us what your mother suggested.
